questions.question: Which statement most accurately represents the evidence for nature and mental health?
options: Nature is proven to treat mental illness in randomized controlled trials
Nature exposure is associated with mental health benefits in observational studies, with weaker intervention evidence
Nature has no measurable psychological effects beyond placebo
All types of nature exposure produce identical mental health effects
questions.explanation: The evidence shows that nature exposure is *associated with* mental health benefits in large observational studies (moderate-to-strong evidence), but intervention evidence (RCTs) is significantly weaker. This means we can't claim nature 'treats' mental illness, only that it's linked to better outcomes in many studies.

questions.question: Which of the three pillars of evidence has the STRONGEST combined evidence base for action-ready mental wellness practices?
options: Stress physiology recovery (cortisol reduction)
Green exercise (outdoor physical activity in nature)
Restorative environments (Attention Restoration Theory)
All three pillars have equally strong evidence
questions.explanation: Green exercise has the strongest combined evidence base because it combines robust exercise science (strong evidence) with nature-specific additive effects (moderate evidence). Outdoor physical activity produces large effect sizes for anxiety, mood, and fatigue — making it the most action-ready domain in nature-based mental wellness.

questions.question: What is the critical limitation of adventure therapy evidence for self-guided practice?
options: Adventure therapy has no scientific evidence
Adventure therapy evidence comes primarily from youth populations in clinical settings with professional delivery
Adventure therapy only works for physical fitness, not mental health
Adventure therapy evidence is stronger for adults than for youth
questions.explanation: Adventure therapy shows moderate evidence for youth populations in residential/clinical settings with trained practitioners. This evidence cannot be directly generalized to self-guided adult outdoor activities — professional delivery is essential to the therapeutic mechanism in licensed adventure therapy programs.

questions.question: According to evidence-based standards, what is nature's appropriate role in trauma recovery?
options: Nature can heal trauma without professional intervention
Nature has no role in trauma recovery — only medication and therapy work
Nature may provide supportive relief from trauma symptoms as an adjunct to professional care
Nature is equally effective as EMDR and Trauma-Focused CBT for PTSD treatment
questions.explanation: The evidence supports nature-based activities as *adjunctive* — they may provide supportive relief from trauma symptoms within a professional care framework, but they do not replace evidence-based trauma treatments like TF-CBT, EMDR, CPT, or PE therapy. 'Nature heals trauma' overstates available evidence and may delay professional care.

questions.question: Which theoretical framework explains why natural environments restore depleted attention through fascination and psychological distance?
options: Stress Reduction Theory (Ulrich)
Biophilia Hypothesis (Wilson)
Attention Restoration Theory (Kaplan & Kaplan)
Self-Determination Theory
questions.explanation: Attention Restoration Theory (ART) by Kaplan & Kaplan proposes that natural environments restore directed attentional capacity through four components: being away (psychological distance), fascination (effortless attention capture), extent (environmental richness), and compatibility (fit with personal goals). Evidence is moderate, with support for working memory, cognitive flexibility, and attentional control improvements.
